title: Peter E. Freeman
text:
I am an associate teaching professor within, and the director of the undergraduate program of, Carnegie Mellon’s Department of Statistics & Data Science.
My primary pedagogical interests are the teaching of mathematical statistics (the calculus-based probability and statistical inference class for undergraduates) and statistical learning (the modeling of data).
I am in the process of authoring two textbooks:

Modern Probability and Statistical Inference (Illustrated with R)
Basic Statistical Learning: a Guide for the Perplexed, with Sara Colando, Erin Franke, and Ron Yurko

This webpage is under construction.

title: Peter E. Freeman
text:
I no longer work in the area of astrostatistics. Some of my past work includes:

Galaxy Morphology

Using random forest to detect galaxies with disturbed morphologies
Using local two-sample testing to detect differences in morphological statistic distributions for binary classes of galaxies (e.g., high-mass v. low-mass, simulated v. real)

Photometric Redshift Estimation

Using spectral connectivity analysis to estimate photometric redshifts
Providing methods for estimating redshifts in the presence of selection bias

Cosmic Microwave Background

Checking the effect of CMB map-making parameters upon observed CMB asymmetries

Nonlinear Data Transformation: using diffusion map to . . .

. . . estimate spectroscopic redshifts of SDSS galaxies and to detect outliers
. . . estimate photometric redshifts of SDSS and DEEP2 galaxies
. . . estimate properties of SDSS galaxies

Supernovae

Dark energy inference
Inferring the velocity field of the local universe
Semi-supervised methodology for classification
